title: vimeo_1176711960
displayTitle: 2026-05-13 - Governance and Administration Committee - Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues
topic: Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues
description: Wednesday 13 May 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues Fire and Emergency New Zealand Rebecca Keoghan, Board Chair Danny Tuato’o, Deputy Board Chair Kerry Gregory, Chief Executive Megan Stiffler, Deputy Chief Executive Operational Response / Deputy National Commander Darryl Purdy, DCE Corporate Services Jim Stuart-Black, Acting DCE Assets and Programme Delivery Ken Cooper, National Manager Response Capability Becks Kearns, Chief of Staff
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues. Fire and Emergency New Zealand. Rebecca Keoghan, Board Chair

chunks.text: Koto. Welcome to the DNA select committee this morning. And before we start, can I say thank you very much for the information that you sent over. I had a good look at that last night, as I'm sure everybody else has done as well, and this is a helpful size as well. So thank you for that, and thank you for your attendance here.

We have 75 minutes with you today, split over the two sessions. Unfortunately, that's the way that that had to go. Yeah. But I understand that you have a presentation to make first, a short one. So I'm going to hand to you, and then I'll be opening up for questions from members. I should say it's good to see you again. Thank you. Thank you, Madam Chair, and kio koto members and committee today. So Fire and Emergency welcomes this inquiry, and we thank the committee for the time to appear today.

I have with me my Chief Executive, Kerry Gregory, to help answer some questions. To the right, Deputy Chief Executive Megan Stifler, who can help us with our fleet deployment processes. And on my left, CFO Darrell Purdy for all things financial. We are here today to provide a detailed view of the state of our fleet, its past and its present state.

We will bring you some more detail on maintenance at the next appearance, and we can take some high-level appearance discussions today, but we are primarily focused on the fleet at this time. We also know that you're particularly concerned about the Type Threes. You've also asked us questions on the wider fleet, and the pack in front of you provides the full picture of all of that fleet.

And I'd like to set the scene to help you understand the material and frame up some of the issues that are involved. But firstly, let's acknowledge our paid and volunteer firefighters. And in the year to June 2025, our crews attended 88,805 incidents across the country. The purpose of our fleet is simple, getting our crews to those emergencies quickly and safely, and we understand the frustration when an appliance breaks down or is unavailable, which is why we aspire to bring the average age of our fleet down.

I also want to acknowledge that some previous answers to the committee have created confusion. There was never any intention to mislead, but some of our answers were incomplete, incorrect, or unclear, and some of our technical terms were not properly explained. For that, we apologize. Today, our aim is to provide the clearest factual picture that we can.

There are three points I would just like to emphasize. First, the operating reality. Fire and Emergency operate one of New Zealand's largest heavy fleets. It's specialized, nationally dispersed, and operates in more than 600 sites. When Fire and Emergency was established in 2017, it inherited a complex and aged fleet environment.

This is not an excuse, but it is an important context. Since Fire and Emergency was established, more than 300 appliances have been put on the run after going through checks and crew training. The second point is safety. We know the impact of outages. To provide some reassurance, last year across those 88,000 deployments, there were 246 incidents that involved an unplanned outage that kept an appliance from attending.

But when that does happen, we have a range of network and operational responses that can manage the situation, and Megan can help us answer some of those questions on that later.The third point is transparency and improvement. There are positive fleet stories. A renewal across several of our appliance types and rebuilding the rural fleet is a positive story. But the focus now is understandably on the Type 3s and the Aerials.

For our Type 3s, we accept that there have been serious delays. On our Aerials, the age profile is a concern. Our response to these challenges has been to strengthen our governance and reporting, tighter tracking, better information, and additional independent reassurance for the management and the board. But we see this inquiry as an opportunity to help improve the transparency between the committee and Fire and Emergency for future decision-making. So Madam Chair, we are here in that spirit today, open, factual, constructive. We agree with the need to

reduce the age of our fleet, whilst recognizing that we do have funding constraints. And we want to give the committee reassurance that Fire and Emergency is serious about the work. So thank you for the brief opening, and we'll now hand back to you for questions. Thank you, Rebecca. I'm going to go straight away to the first question from Tim. Thank you. Thank you very much, Board Chair, Commander, and team for fronting. So, I think over the course of the next few weeks, there are obviously going to be a number of questions about the fleet of appliances we have,

suitability, can they be built in New Zealand versus the offshore ones, sustainment, replacement, interaction to service, some of those things. But we'll have plenty of time today. I'd just like to start with some sort of broader overview in line with the comments you've just made, and then we can have a chance to maybe drill into the Type 3, just as one example- Sure... to understand, I guess, fleet processes, because as you say, it's been quite topical.

I think one of the aims of this is just to increase public and parliamentary confidence in FENZ. Nothing is going to fix vehicles or magic up new vehicles overnight. But I think it presents an opportunity to build confidence in FENZ and leadership, and to demonstrate that the issues are well understood, that plans are in place, even if they take time to work through, so.

But as you say, we're here in large part because of the conflicting, concerning, and confusing answers that we've been given over the last few hearings, particularly with that tranche two of MAN cabs. We held an extra hearing in March because, which is quite unusual in itself. So where were you both at that meeting? Pardon? Where were you both at the last meeting in March?

So we made what we thought at the time was the right decision to send the people with the detail to that meeting, understanding that you wanted further detail specifically around those areas and issues that had been discussed in the previous meeting. And given the short timeframe, we assumed that that would be the right people to send. So in hindsight, we know that was not received well, but that was our decision at the time.

There was a perception that those staff got thrown under the bus. How do you respond to that? Wasn't the intention at all. They are the experts in that field, and they have the detail. Did it bring the clarification that we were hoping for? Well, given that we're here today, no. I think we've got to give you more, and we need to help take the committee on the journey for that.

So as board chair, have you been asking the same questions that we've been asking as a select committee? Absolutely. The board in its entirety, long before the inquiry was commenced, have worked hard on the fleet progress. We have a financial and investment sustainability committee, which I'm pretty sure I've talked to you about before, which has taken part of the fleet deep dive into that subcommittee.

And we are aware of the challenges. We're also aware of our fiscal responsibility and the envelope in which we are operating. We've spent a lot of time as an organization sitting up asset management plans to make sure we are looking at our fleet in an appropriate way, and spent a lot of time looking at what is appropriate for the organization. So you've been asking the same questions. Have you been getting the same answers?

Yes. And you're not concerned about answers that we've been given? We are concerned. No, but are you concerned that the answers we've been getting are wrong and you've been getting the same answers? So there was definitely some confusion at those previous meetings, and I think, Kerry, you've got some clarification on that. Would you like to go through that, particularly on those MAN 3s and the 30 trucks?

We'll come to that in a minute. But my question is, you're not concerned that the answers we're getting is wrong and- I'm concerned that there's been some confusion in the answers that are given, but the answers that we've, or questions that we have asked at board level and subcommittee level, we've had the appropriate answers for. So that's why we need to- But you must have sat there and said, "Hang on a minute. I think the wrong message is coming across." Because as you've just said, you understood the situation. So surely at that point, either

you're going to jump in or you're going to write to the committee afterwards and go, "Hang on a minute. I think we got that one wrong." And that hasn't happened. There was some correspondence back to the committee, which we talked about. We clarified certain things. Yes. Yeah, absolutely. Was concerned about the confusion, for sure. Okay.

Right. So let's just take one example then. We've obviously heard a bit about are there any trucks on the road. I think we all understand today none of the tranche two trucks are on the road, but they will be by July. Not all 30, but the first lot will be by July. I can take you through the whole process, if you like, through that.

Certainly, our aim is to have them on by July, but there are some issues that we're dealing with that may push them past July, but they'll certainly be on- Sure. But let's just take as one example, how many in that first wave that we were hoping for July, plus or minus the issues that might delay it a few, but- So the 16 that, if you have a look at the green sheet here that we supplied.

Yeah. But it is 16 or 17. Well, it might pay if I take you through the context to clarify the confusion. Yeah. Because we have got stuck in the past in giving the committee real clarity on actually the numbers and the makeup between those. So I think if you- If you allow me just to give you a quick context of how that evolved, it'll bring a lot more clarity across the numbers. That would be helpful.

Yeah. But just before we do that. Yeah. Here's the reason I'm asking this. At the last meeting, the one that we're here for, we had this exact question of was it '16 or '17. Yeah. And I thought it was pretty clear from your pre-hearing answers and your post-hearing and your review answers that it was 17, including the trial appliance of the AngloCo Man Cab Tranche 2, pipe three.

And I was told, "No, no, no. It's '16." And I specifically said, "Are you sure it's not '17?" The written answer here say '17, very clear. And it's not '16. And if the culture, we'll start with the board because we're going to come to officer command in a minute. But if the culture within the board is, "No, no, we're out of transparency, we're putting everything right."

And you can see something as clear as that where I specifically say, "Is it '16 or '17?" "No, it's only '16." What's happened to the other tranche? And we can't get an answer. Surely at that point, you could just send us a note and go, "Well, it's 16 are the ones that were built up. So last year, plus there's the trial appliance. That's the 17th."

Mm-hmm. Better referring to that. We're just not seeing that kind of proactive approach. Sure. Understood. And I think that's what's creating concern. Yeah. But I'm not seeing a proactive approach out of the boards who are appointed as the public's representatives to oversee things from a government position. Sure. So today we have the answers to those.

And I think I'd like, if possible to, is the team- No, that's good... to go through that. I'm great. But we shouldn't have had to come here today to get those answers, should we? No. So my question to the board is what have you been doing all this time? So we've spent, as I talked you through before, a lot of time looking at fleet. And we are aware of some of the challenges in terms of the miscommunication at this committee level. Hence the reason we need the opportunity now to give you this detail, and we would really like that opportunity to take you through, particularly

this era, so we can make sure that we are all on the same page and that we can move forward. Thank you. We'll do that now. Okay. Thank you. Gary, if you want to take us through that. Yeah. And some of that lack of clarity has come from bouncing the numbers, and from our side, bouncing the numbers, when we were very into different phases or aspects of it.

And that's why I want to give some real clarity to it. So in 2019, we ordered 30 Man cab chassis. And then in October 2019, we identified the structural body cracking in 28 of the 47 Man Fraser type threes, which had been operationally deployed at that time from previous procurement.

So following that, decision was made to pause the further production of any type threes until a solution had been identified for the cracking. The pause is actually quite pivotal in the long term of what's, then this process has dragged on for multiple years. So just to be clear on that point then, can you just give me the date, right back at the start, when did Tranche 2 start?

Tranche 2, so the 30 appliances? Yes. So in August 2019, we ordered the 30 Man cab chassis. Right. So it didn't start at the end of 2023? I'll get to that, the phase in '23. No, no, I just want to confirm that when Tranche 2 started, so it was 2019.

Well, it depends what- The slide in '21 would be helpful as well, Mr. Costa, if that page gives you the timeline. Oh, yeah, I just had someone draw me up one, and it starts- Yeah... here nicely at December 2023. So in '23, what we did at that stage, and there's a step that comes before that, so I would appreciate if you'd let me go through it because it will become clear.

Yeah. But in '23, that's when we went to AngloCo to design further 28 build. So 28 of those chassis that were bought in 2019. Of the 30 chassis, 28 of them we went to AngloCo to design those, and that's what you're referring to as Tranche 2.

So it started at '23 or 2019? That's what I'm saying, that's- Started in 2019. That's why we need to go through in a sequential point or it won't, it'll become confusing. Yeah. So 2019- It does, because do you recognize this? I do recognize that. Yeah. It's what you gave us at the last hearing. Yep. To kind of assuage our concerns, and it specifically says, "Tranche 2 starts in December 2023."

That is when we gave the- Chassis... 28 chassis to AngloCo to design the appliances that are coming on. So it's skipped four years of history, hasn't it? Is that- It specifically says it starts in... There's a little code, Tranche 2. It's only Tranche 1 up until here.

Yeah. So what I'm trying to do today is give the committee real clarity around the timeline to put you through that. So not going back at what's been supplied in the past, but giving, and not saying that's wrong, it's actually filling the gaps where there are gaps in there. Okay. It's not wrong, it's just not been answered.

So but can you, I think you've skipped a bit. So you got to, you had the cracking issue. Yep. In the meantime, you were also running your next generation type three project, and you've gone out for RFPs for that. Mm-hmm. So how did you select AngloCo in 2023 to build the remaining 28?

Okay, so following that decision to pause the type threes. Mm-hmm. And that took longer than anticipated. It wasn't until 2021 that a decision was made to send one of the 30 Man chassis that we'd bought in 2019 to AngloCo, and one of those chassis to SEM. And that was to design a different prototype for the body build through there.

But how are they selected? So that was two of those originally 30 Man chassis. Yeah, but how were SEM and AngloCo selected? Oh, they've selected through the procurement process to sort of identify- They're the ones that responded to an RFP or something, did they? I'd have to come back with that detail. I haven't gone into the detail of what happened back at that stage.

Does anyone in the room know? Because as I understand- Darryl should know. Yes, Darryl has some notes on it. Did SCM put in an RFP for the next generation? So, the process we undertook was, because we had a panel for the next generation Type 3s, we sought external advice, and following that advice, we approached a few of the suppliers who were on the panel for the next generation Type 3 and asked them if they could rapidly fill the build on the main chassis so that we wouldn't have a gap. The intention was we wouldn't have a gap in the production Type 3, so that's

how that occurred. We sought advice and followed that advice. So that makes sense. But SCM definitely then had put in an RFP for next generation Type 3. Correct. So separate process, if you like. But we're drawing on that panel of suppliers that we've gone to market for, for the next generations to do this short-term build on the main chassis. So any information that said that there'd only been four RFPs being Scania, AngloCo, Scania NZ with Fraser Fire, Emergency One, but not SCM, that SCM had not put in a RFP for the next generation, that would be incorrect, would it?

I'd need to come back to you on that. Right. Because otherwise, it just kind of looked like, get the AngloCo piece, they'd put in an RFP. But if SCM hadn't, it looks like they've been plucked out of thin air. I'd need to clarify that detail with you. Happy to do that. Okay. Great. Thank you. Mm-hmm. Okay, and with that, can we just finish this chronology?

Yeah, can we just finish this bit off? Yeah. Thank you. Yeah. So, obviously at that stage, we went out to those two suppliers and then provided them one of each of those 30 chassis. And so then in 2023, we went to AngloCo's design and asked them to build a further 28.

So that was after seeing the design elements through that. So- Do you test them as part of that when you selected AngloCo from the two? You've now got two trial appliances. Mm-hmm. Do they get tested? My understanding was the delays that were taken with SCM were just too long in actually being able to bring a prototype to fruition.

And the contract was then provided to AngloCo on the basis of the design that they'd provided through the... That could meet the spec. So the AngloCo one wasn't tested before it was selected. I would have to check that detail, too. Do you know how long that AngloCo test one spent in the workshops over the two years after it arrived broken?

Do you know, Darryl? No, I don't. No, we'd have to come back to that too. We don't know that either. Okay. Okay. So that was back in 2023. And so they were asked to build a further 28, and that's where the number changes from the 30 to the 28 because two are in prototypes.

Of those 28, we received 16 appliances, which are going through the various stages to allow them to be put into operational use. And that was 16 that are shown here above the black line. So they have the 16. The two below the black line are the two prototypes. To be clear, right now, of all of those appliances, none of them are in operational use.

They're still, as you can see from the lines and the process they go through, in various stages of the pipe. So the remaining 12, so that's 18 from the 16 under 2. So the remaining 12 from those original 30, they haven't been received yet by foreign emergencies.

What that means is we haven't taken ownership of those appliances. They're still going through the build process. They're in New Zealand. I traveled up to Auckland myself to have a look at those and we cite that the 12 are in New Zealand, but they're still going through that build phase before we will accept ownership of those.

I know you're going to ask me about the timeline for those 12 as well. I need to make sure that before we take ownership that we're balancing up the expediency and also making the contractual suitability of those. So we want to make sure that all of the contractual obligations actually have been met before we receive and take ownership of those things.

And we have found through our operational deployment checks as we take them through from receiving them to put them into operational use, a number of issues that have come out through there that currently are being rectified by the supplier. So we're actually holding the supplier to account to rectify those issues.

It gives me assurance that our operational process that we go through before we put them in deployment from when we receive them is picking up those, and the supplier's working really proactively and positively with us in rectifying any of those issues that are coming up. And the SCM trial truck, will that go into service?

The SCM trial truck, it will go into service. Currently, that's destined to go to New Plymouth. And the- Does it come with all the sort of requisite manuals and things that you'd expect? Do the what, sorry? Come with all the sort of requisite manuals for the appliance that you would expect?

I don't have that detail, but I'll certainly assume it would. Okay. I'm just going to check, Melissa, was yours a sub? A sub, yeah. Okay. So does that mean I- Yeah, you can go now. Thank you. Thank you, Madam Chair. I just want to go back to the two prototypes that you actually talked about. So they have actually gone out to the two different companies to build.

Correct. And you count the 16. So you actually count, hang on, two chassis. So out of the 30, you now are left with 22 because you've got two prototypes. What happens to those two prototypes? Even though they were actually gone out to be built by two separate companies- They're still going to be used... do they actually come back to you as part of the fleet?

Yes. They do, yeah. So one of those prototypes will be going to Rotorua and one will be going to New Plymouth. So they are actually coming back, so that is included. So you now have 12 that haven't actually been received by FENZ. Correct. Sir, you actually initially talked about the concern for the age of the fleet. Mm-hmm. So when we have actually received the vehicle, we've ordered it in 2019, you haven't actually received this 12, and none of these vehicles are operational. So how old would you class those vehicle as when you're calculating the age of the fleet?

Oh, so- So are they- Yeah. It's up to you. Yeah. So this is 2026. You ordered them in 2019. Mm-hmm. It's been in the country for seven years, and it's aged, as far as most people are concerned, when you purchase a car or anything else. So how would you classify their age when you're actually calculating the age of the fleet?

We'd classify the age of it from when we receive it, which is when the total build is finished. So the appliances that aren't actually fully built and compliant and ready to be deployed on the road until they're built. I would say that's the same of most vehicles. So if you would actually... I'll give you an example.

If you purchase a car on the market right now and you actually parked it in the garage for seven years, how old would you actually classify that car as? It's too- And what I'd say- Would you call it brand new? Or would you call it seven years old? If I broke down the components of that car, I would have no idea of how old the No, that wasn't the question. You purchase a car today, brand new, no, back in 2019, and you parked it in the car and not used it, and how old would you classify that car as?

Would you call it brand new or would you call it seven years old? Well, I think we're not comparing apples with apples. Well, it's a vehicle. It's a machinery that hasn't been used. It's still in the build phase. There are technologies that progress. It's seven years. Yeah. So you're calling it brand new, are you?

I would class a vehicle brand new from the time that it's completed its build process. Wow. Yes. But it's not the build, it's not the vehicle that's being built, it's actually- We've got another couple of subs. Somebody hasn't finished the car in your garage, that's the issue. We've got a couple of subs. So one from Andy and then one from Mike, and then I'm going to- Yeah, look, and tell me whether you want me to come back to this.

No. So just having gone now through this document that you've given us, if you look at the age of the fleet- Yeah... basically what you're saying is you're keeping up with Type 1, 2 rural tankers, special fleets. They're more or less the same. But in the aerials and Type 3s, they're just going worse- Yes... the whole time, and it's gotten worse and worse- Yep... at the moment. So I think what we're looking for is some maturity.

We got to actually get on top of that because I think the number of vehicles you have commissioned, Type 3s you've commissioned, is four, is it? What page was that on? But since 2017- Yeah... you've essentially commissioned virtually nothing. Yeah. Type 3s. The Type 3s, yes. Yeah. Okay. So then you've got some information later on in this about essentially the funding deficit.

Mm-hmm. Can you just talk us through what that actually means? So you've got these investment gaps for all appliances. Mm-hmm. Can you just walk us through what that graph actually means? Andy, would it be possible to come back to that because- Well, I was wondering whether you want to come back- Yeah. I think-... because this is quite a big area for you... I think that, yes, it is.

Okay. No, I finally- So can we come back to that? And- But I do want to come back to that because that's- Yes. We'll hold that thought. No, can we hold that- That's the heart of it. Yeah... and come back to it? Yeah. Because I think that we need to carry on with what we were talking about. Okay. I just want to get to a high level. No, that's all good. Thank you. Mike. Thank you. I just want to quickly touch on, I guess, the cab chassis that were ordered in 2000, or arrived in 2019, and obviously are still sitting there.

And my question around that is just around the warranty for those chassis. And actually, what is the warranty of it? And are we basically seven years through that? And how does that affect it? Have you got the info on the warranty? No. Yeah. We will have to come back to you on that.

Haven't got the detail of- Specific... what you need today, the specifics, and I don't want to lead the committee astray with incorrect information. Yeah. Sorry, but that's not really good enough, is it? The board chair sat here and said we've counted eight, specifically a carrier to Type 3. Yep. You can't tell me if it's the input in a RFP is, and how they were chosen. You can't tell Mr.

Davidson, if the warranty is in use. You can't tell us exactly how it's passed. There is- Isn't that the point of today? Yeah. The warranty is in place. What I don't want to give the detail on is the specifics of when the warranty started, and that's the question that I'm hearing from Mr. Davidson.

Yeah. When it started, when it expires. Yeah. And the insurance. Yeah. So I want to be accurate on that with the committee. I don't want to guess and revert to memory as to what that says. So the only reason I'm not giving the exact clarity is I want to be precise because we've confused the committee before by incorrect or misses or information.

It's true. It's not an excuse to avoid the question though, is it? I'm not avoiding the question. It's not avoiding the question. But we'll come back with the answer. Just want to be accurate with the answer. Thank you. Okay, Lamonga, and then I'll come to Andy if there's no more subs around the- Thank you. I've been really listening intently to the questions, but also your answers, because it's really important to understand the operating reality and specifically how, over time, the new trucks have arrived, then they haven't been in operation.

So in terms of the operating reality- Yeah... because this process, we've received a number of submissions from very concerned, not just members of the public, but also your workforce. Mm-hmm. So I want to go back to the operating reality, where you've got hundreds of sites and then you've got this problem of workforce requiring the equipment, and you haven't got fire trucks in.

All of us have received lots of concerns around the breakdowns, not just from the workforce, specifically from the public. Mm. What is the strategy moving forward? You are going to come back to us with specific data that's going to be helpful. What is the operating reality from here on in towards the end of the year? Because I'm really concerned when you have truck breakdowns, you've got firefighters sitting on the side of the road waiting for things to be updated.

So can you help us understand what is the contingency plan that you have in place? Not just the numbers, but the reality, the operating reality which the Chair openly incomes. Sure. Yeah. That's a better question. Yeah. So with regard to that, we don't minimize the impact at a local level at all. Both Megan and myself have been operational firefighters. We understand, when an appliance breaks down, how devastating that is at a local level around being able to respond to support your community.

So, that's not minimized at all. There's two things I'll get Megan to speak in a minute about our network, because we don't operate on a station-by-station basis. We operate on a network basis, which ensures that we can respond to communities. But I do take you to this slide number 13 in your pack.

And the reason for this slide in the pack isn't to belittle the impact at a local level, it's to actually show at a system level what it looks like. So 99.72% of the time, we respond to the calls, and that's based from 2001 to 2025, which shows the impact of any mechanical issue you've experienced between the time of a call is given at a station and the time that it responds back to get on station and recommission again.

So what this is trying to show is the reality of the system actually working in place rather than any given appliance breaking down at any given time. So the high-level statement that I would make in there is that mechanical breakdowns don't equate to inability to service communities and to respond to appliances.

This is limited to that period. It doesn't mean that there's not other appliances that are unavailable at that time. It's about a system level network. Megan, do you just want to talk a little bit about what happens when appliance does break down in these slides? Sure. So what you're seeing here is those figures relate to appliances that have been assigned to a working job. And what Kerry has pointed out, the CE has pointed out, is 99.72% of the time, those vehicles that are assigned to a job turn out to that job.

If a vehicle, and in this instance, you can see there were 98 times that an appliance, in 2025, couldn't go out of the station. There are very few occurrences in our paid network where only one appliance is assigned to a job. So we have 1,267 appliances across our whole country.

That is a network of appliances, and very few times is only one appliance sent. So if in those 98 times a truck couldn't turn out, there is already another truck en route. Now, that doesn't mean it is not frustrating for us. There is nothing worse as a firefighter to hop into a truck and you can't roll out the door. I understand that.

But when we talk about serving our community, we've got a network, and there are many reasons a truck can't be assigned. They could be at an ongoing job, so we have to use the network to get to the next job. So if you imagine 1,200 trucks placed across the country, if we need 10 of them to go to a factory fire, we don't leave the station that they've gone from vacant.

We move trucks in and cover that. We create a coverage network. I think what we're seeing here is a result of the age of our fleet. The plans we have in play for procurement is recognizing the older the fleet is, the more you have to maintain it, and we need to get those numbers down.

We hear our firefighters, and I agree with them. I am a firefighter. The age of the fleet needs to be reduced. Can I ask a sub to that, please? On this graph, and I read this last night and I see that it says there the results need to be treated with care, but it is going up. And what does 2026 show in comparison? So what are the data this year? Is it going up as well?

Is it increasing than- We can bring that data back to the committee. But the essence is as a fleet gets older, it's going to require more maintenance. Like Megan said, we're very wholeheartedly, we need to bring the age profile of our fleet down, which will reduce the maintenance of the fleet as a result of that.

But we'll certainly expect to see that graph continuing to rise as we try to bring that- Until it comes down. Okay, thank you. Jim. He's got a sub on that. Thank you. Yeah. What are your performance metrics for the supplier? How often do you review and report them, and how are they performing? That's a good question. Yeah, so we can bring you the detail around that if you want the detail on that, but we certainly hold our suppliers account to delivery. There's been multiple issues through there with the suppliers and the delivery and
